title: Burglary as defined in s9 Theft Act 1968
summary: 9084 Criminal — s9(1)(a) and (b): entry as trespasser with intent or having entered.
learningObjectives: Analyse trespass and mens rea at time of entry.
Apply cases: R v Collins, R v Brown, R v Ryan.

simpleExplanation.title: Breaking in with intent
simpleExplanation.summary: Burglary is entering a building or part of one as a trespasser — either intending to steal/inflict GBH/unlawful damage, or having stolen or attempted.
steps: Trespass — without permission or exceeding it.
s9(1)(a) — intent at entry; (b) — offence inside.

flashcards.back: Enters building/part as trespasser with intent to steal, inflict GBH, or do unlawful damage.
Having entered as trespasser, steals/attempts or inflicts/attempts GBH.
Effective entry — part of body (or instrument attached to D) must intrude; jury decides if sufficient.
Entry possible even if D stuck halfway through window and stole nothing — effective entry still possible.
Entry without permission or exceeding permission given — mens rea at time of entry (R v Jones and Smith).
Counter area of shop closed to public — D exceeded implied permission by entering that part.

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between burglary under s9(1)(a) and s9(1)(b) of the Theft Act 1968?
flashcards.back: s9(1)(a) requires intent to commit an ulterior offence *at the time of entry*. The crime is complete upon entry. s9(1)(b) involves committing or attempting an ulterior offence *after* having entered as a trespasser.

flashcards.front: What is the current legal test for 'entry' in burglary, as established in R v Ryan (1996)?
flashcards.back: Any part of the defendant's body entering the building is sufficient to constitute an 'entry', even if they are physically unable to proceed further or commit the intended crime.

flashcards.front: How can a person with permission to enter a building still be a trespasser for the purposes of burglary?
flashcards.back: By exceeding their permission. In R v Jones and Smith (1976), entering with an unlawful purpose (to steal) went beyond the general permission given, making the entry a trespass.

flashcards.front: What is the two-part mens rea required for burglary?
flashcards.back: 1. Intention or subjective recklessness as to being a trespasser. 2. The mens rea for the relevant ulterior offence (e.g., intention to steal for theft).

flashcards.front: Which ulterior offences apply to s9(1)(a) and s9(1)(b) respectively?
flashcards.back: s9(1)(a): Theft, Grievous Bodily Harm (GBH), or Criminal Damage. s9(1)(b): Theft or Grievous Bodily Harm (GBH) only. Criminal Damage is excluded from s9(1)(b).

sections.content: **Burglary s9** (2.2.3) — distinguish **(a) vs (b)** and when **mens rea** must exist. For (a), intent must exist **at entry**; for (b), the ulterior offence follows entry as trespasser.
The Two Forms of Burglary: s9(1)(a) and s9(1)(b)

Section 9 of the Theft Act 1968 creates two distinct ways of committing burglary. The first, under s9(1)(a), is committed when a person enters a building (or part of a building) as a trespasser with intent to commit one of the ulterior offences: theft, grievous bodily harm (GBH), or criminal damage. The offence is complete at the moment of entry, regardless of whether the intended crime is actually carried out. The second form, under s9(1)(b), occurs when a person, having already entered a building as a trespasser, then proceeds to commit or attempt to commit theft or GBH. Here, the offence is complete when the defendant commits or attempts the ulterior act. The key distinction lies in the timing of the mens rea for the ulterior offence.

items: s9(1)(a): Enters as a trespasser with intent. The crime is complete upon entry.
s9(1)(b): Having entered as a trespasser, commits or attempts an offence. The crime is complete upon the commission or attempt.
The ulterior offences differ: s9(1)(a) includes criminal damage, whereas s9(1)(b) does not.
Understanding which subsection applies is crucial for analysing problem scenarios correctly.

sections.content: The actus reus of burglary requires 'entry' into a building 'as a trespasser'. The term 'entry' is not defined in the Act, so its meaning has been developed through case law. The initial test in R v Collins (1973) required an 'effective and substantial' entry. However, this has been superseded. In R v Brown (1985), a defendant leaning through a broken shop window was deemed to have entered. The current authority, R v Ryan (1996), established a very low threshold: the defendant was found guilty when his head and arm became stuck in a window, and he was unable to steal anything. This confirms that the entry of any part of the defendant's body into the building is sufficient. The entry must also be as a 'trespasser', meaning without consent or legal authority.

items: The meaning of 'entry' is defined by common law, not statute.
The modern test from R v Ryan (1996) is that entry of any part of the body is sufficient.
The previous 'effective and substantial' test from R v Collins (1973) is no longer the leading authority.
A trespasser is someone who enters without the express or implied permission of the person in control of the property.

sections.content: Actus Reus: Exceeding Permission and 'Part of a Building'

A person can become a trespasser even if they initially had permission to enter a building. This occurs when they exceed the terms of that permission. In R v Jones and Smith (1976), the defendant had general permission to be in his father's house but was convicted of burglary because he entered with the intention to steal, which was outside the scope of his permission. The concept of entering 'part of a building' as a trespasser is also vital. In R v Walkington (1979), the defendant was a lawful visitor in a department store but became a trespasser when he went behind a counter into an area closed to the public with the intent to steal from the till. This demonstrates that one can be a lawful visitor in one part of a building but a trespasser in another.

items: Entering with an unlawful purpose can negate permission, making the entry a trespass (R v Jones and Smith).
A person can be a trespasser by entering a restricted area within a building they are otherwise lawfully in (R v Walkington).
The mens rea for trespass is knowledge or subjective recklessness as to being a trespasser (R v Collins).

sections.content: In problem questions, always identify the exact point of entry and whether the defendant had permission to be in that specific part of the building. If they exceed their permission, they become a trespasser, and you must state this clearly, citing R v Jones and Smith or R v Walkington.

The mens rea for burglary is complex as it has two distinct parts. First, the defendant must have the mens rea for the initial trespass. This means they must either know they are a trespasser or be subjectively reckless as to whether they are trespassing. An honest but mistaken belief that they have permission to enter means they are not a trespasser. Second, the defendant must have the mens rea for the ulterior offence. For s9(1)(a), this is the intention to commit theft, GBH, or criminal damage, formed at the point of entry. Importantly, conditional intent (e.g., intending to steal 'anything of value') is sufficient, as confirmed in AG's Reference (Nos 1 and 2 of 1979). For s9(1)(b), the mens rea for theft or GBH is formed after entry.

items: Part 1 of Mens Rea: Intention or subjective recklessness as to trespassing.
Part 2 of Mens Rea: Intention for the ulterior offence.
For s9(1)(a), this intention must exist upon entry.
Conditional intent to steal is sufficient for the mens rea of s9(1)(a) burglary.

sections.question: Leo enters a shop during opening hours and walks behind the counter (staff-only area) to steal cash from the till. Analyse burglary under s9(1)(a) and (b). [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**1. Building (s9(2)):** A shop is a **building** — includes inhabited vehicles/vessels; a fixed shop clearly qualifies.

**2. Entry:** Leo **entered** the shop — *R v Collins* requires effective entry; walking through the door satisfies this. For the **counter area**, *R v Walkington* — entering a part of a building the public is not invited into can be entry as trespasser even if D lawfully entered the building overall.

**3. Trespasser:** Leo had implied permission to enter as a **customer**, not to go **behind the counter** — he **exceeded** permission (*R v Jones and Smith*: permission can be withdrawn or exceeded). He is a **trespasser** when entering the restricted area.

**4. s9(1)(a) — intent at entry:** When Leo entered the staff area, did he **intend to steal**? If he formed intent **before** entering that part, s9(1)(a) is satisfied — intent to steal, inflict GBH, or cause unlawful damage at time of entry.

**5. s9(1)(b) — ulterior offence:** If Leo entered the counter area as trespasser and **then stole** (or attempted), s9(1)(b) applies — the theft need not have been intended at initial entry to the shop, only that he was a trespasser when the theft occurred inside.

**Conclusion:** Leo is likely guilty of **s9(1)(a)** if intent existed at entry to the restricted area, and/or **s9(1)(b)** when he stole from the till. Cite *Walkington* for exceeding implied permission.

faq.q: Does a burglar have to steal something for the crime of burglary to be complete?
faq.a: No. For a s9(1)(a) burglary, the offence is complete the moment the defendant enters a building as a trespasser with the *intent* to steal, cause GBH, or commit criminal damage. The ulterior crime does not need to be committed. For a s9(1)(b) burglary, they must *attempt* or commit theft or GBH, but a completed theft is not required; an attempt is sufficient.

faq.q: If someone enters a shop legally but then hides in a stockroom to steal, which type of burglary is it?
faq.a: This would be charged under s9(1)(a). Although they entered the main building legally, they became a trespasser when they entered the 'part of a building' (the stockroom) where they had no permission to be, as in R v Walkington. At that point of entry into the restricted area, they had the intent to steal, satisfying all the elements of s9(1)(a).

faq.q: What is 'conditional intent' and is it enough for burglary?
faq.a: Conditional intent is when a defendant intends to steal only if they find something worth taking. The case of Attorney-General's Reference (Nos 1 and 2 of 1979) confirmed that this is sufficient for the mens rea of a s9(1)(a) burglary. The defendant does not need to have a specific item in mind; a general intention to steal whatever is valuable is enough.

comparisonTable.caption: Comparison of Burglary under s9(1)(a) and s9(1)(b)

cells: Upon entry into the building or part of a building.
After entry, upon committing or attempting the ulterior offence.
Intent for the ulterior offence must exist *at the time of entry*.
Intent for the ulterior offence is formed *after having entered*.
Theft, Grievous Bodily Harm (GBH), or Criminal Damage.
Theft or Grievous Bodily Harm (GBH). (Criminal Damage is excluded).
The ulterior offence does not need to be committed or even attempted.
The ulterior offence must be either *committed* or *attempted*.
